entry.title: Integrate CAP application as outbound channel for SAP Document AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

When integrating SAP Document AI as a document extraction engine into a broader BTP solution, one of the first hurdles you’ll encounter is the outbound callback mechanism. Document AI supports notifying an external system once extraction is complete — a great feature for building event-driven, asynchronous document processing pipelines.

However, there’s a subtle but important quirk: Document AI unconditionally performs a CSRF token preflight before sending the actual extraction result callback. It fires a GET request with the header X-CSRF-Token: Fetch to your callback endpoint regardless of whether CSRF protection is actually in place on the receiving end.

This behavior cannot be disabled in the Document AI configuration.

The solution? Register Express middleware directly during CAP’s bootstrap phase — before the framework takes over — and handle both the preflight and the callback there.

Architecture Overview

SAP Document AI
      │
      │  ① GET /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: Fetch
      │◄─── X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │
      │  ② POST /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │     Body: notification payload
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CAP Application             │
│                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │     csrf-middleware.js         │  │
│  │  • Issues token on GET/HEAD    │  │
│  │  • Validates token on POST     │  │
│  └───────────────┬────────────────┘  │
│                  │                   │
│  ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐  │
│  │     callback-service.js        │  │
│  │  • Parses extraction payload   │  │
│  │  • Matches & finalises job     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

The middleware intercepts both requests before CAP’s own routing and CSRF logic runs, acting as a transparent proxy layer between Document AI and your business logic.

Implementation Walkthrough

1. Hooking into the CAP Bootstrap

The key to this entire approach is cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => { ... }). This event fires after CAP has initialized the underlying Express application but before it registers its own middleware stack. This gives us direct, unmediated access to the raw Express app object.

The server.js is deliberately thin. Its only responsibility is wiring the CSRF middleware into the Express stack at the right moment:

'use strict';

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const csrfMiddleware = require('./csrf-middleware');

cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    app.use(csrfMiddleware);
});

module.exports = cds.server;

⚠️Exporting cds.server is essential — this tells CAP to use the standard server startup while still respecting your bootstrap customizations.

The actual callback business logic lives separately in callback-service.js, keeping concerns cleanly separated. The middleware handles the HTTP-level CSRF handshake; the service handles what to do with the payload.

2. The CSRF Middleware

The full middleware lives in csrf-middleware.js and handles two responsibilities: issuing tokens on preflight requests and validating tokens on incoming POST requests.

Token Lifecycle

Rather than returning a static or meaningless string, the middleware generates a cryptographically random token per preflight, stores it in memory with a TTL, and enforces single-use consumption:

// In-memory token store: token → expiry timestamp
const _tokens = new Map();
const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 10 * 60 * 1000; // 10 minutes

function generateToken() {
    const token = crypto.randomBytes(24).toString('hex');
    _tokens.set(token, Date.now() + TOKEN_TTL_MS);
    return token;
}

function validateToken(token) {
    if (!token) return false;
    const expiry = _tokens.get(token);
    if (!expiry) return false;
    if (Date.now() > expiry) { _tokens.delete(token); return false; }
    _tokens.delete(token); // single-use: consumed on first valid POST
    return true;
}

A periodic cleanup interval ensures expired tokens don’t accumulate indefinitely:

setInterval(() => {
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const [token, expiry] of _tokens.entries()) {
        if (expiry < now) _tokens.delete(token);
    }
}, 60000);

The Middleware Function

The middleware is path-scoped to /callback/* routes and handles each HTTP method appropriately:

function csrfMiddleware(req, res, next) {
    // Only apply to /callback paths
    if (!req.path.startsWith('/callback')) return next();

    const csrfHeader = req.headers['x-csrf-token'];

    // GET or HEAD preflight: issue a fresh token
    if ((req.method === 'GET' || req.method === 'HEAD') 
            && csrfHeader?.toLowerCase() === 'fetch') {
        const token = generateToken();
        log.debug(`CSRF preflight (${req.method}): issued token for ${req.path}`);
        res.set('X-CSRF-Token', token);
        res.set('Allow', 'GET,HEAD,POST');
        res.set('Cache-Control', 'no-cache, no-store');
        return res.status(200).end();
    }

    // POST: validate the token if present
    if (req.method === 'POST') {
        if (csrfHeader && !validateToken(csrfHeader)) {
            log.warn(`CSRF validation failed for POST ${req.path}`);
            return res.status(403).json({ 
                error: { message: 'CSRF token validation failed' } 
            });
        }
        log.debug(`CSRF token accepted for POST ${req.path}`);
    }

    next();
}

3. Handling the Callback Payload

Once the CSRF handshake is complete, Document AI posts the extraction results to /callback/notify. The actual processing logic is registered as a standard CAP service endpoint in callback-service.js.

What Document AI Sends

This is where it gets interesting. SAP Document AI schemas have a toggle that controls how much data is included directly in the callback payload. Depending on your schema configuration, you’ll encounter two patterns:

Option A — Full extraction result in the callback body

When the "include extraction result" toggle is enabled on your Document AI schema, the callback payload contains the complete extraction result directly in the DocumentResult field — serialized as a Python dictionary string representation (not valid JSON). This means you get all extracted field values, confidence scores, and metadata in a single push, with no need to make a follow-up API call to retrieve results.

DocumentResult = "{file_ID=abc-123, extracted_fields=[...], schema=InvoiceSchema, ...}"

⚠️ Note that this is Python repr() format, not JSON. Key-value pairs use = instead of :, and null values appear as None. Your callback handler needs to account for this — either with a dedicated parser or targeted regex extraction for the fields you need.

Option B — Lightweight notification only

When the toggle is disabled (or a simpler integration is preferred), the callback only contains a minimal notification with the NotificationObjectId. You can then use this ID to fetch the structured extraction result on demand via the Document AI DocumentService:

GET /document-service/v1/DocumentService/Documents({NotificationObjectId})/DocumentService.simpleStructure()

The DocumentService.simpleStructure() endpoint returns a clean, normalized JSON representation of the extracted fields — often the more practical choice if you don’t need the full raw extraction metadata and want a consistent, version-stable data contract between Document AI and your CAP application.

Setting Up the Callback Destination

With the CSRF middleware in place, the next step is making the callback URL reachable from SAP Document AI. This involves two things: defining the destination in your mta.yaml so it is automatically provisioned on deployment (ensuring automatic client credential updates), and then registering it as an outbound channel in the Document AI schema configuration UI.

Defining the Destination in mta.yaml

SAP BTP Destination Service allows you to declare destinations declaratively as part of your MTA deployment descriptor. This means the callback destination is created or updated automatically every time you deploy — no manual destination configuration in the BTP cockpit required.

Add a dedicated content deployer module to your mta.yaml:

# ─── Destination content deployer ─────────────────────────────────────────────
- name: docai-cap-dest-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  requires:
    - name: docai-cap-dest # the name of your destination resource
      parameters:
        content-target: true
    - name: docai-cap-xsuaa # the name of your xsuaa resource
      parameters:
        service-key:
          name: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
    - name: srv-api
  parameters:
    content:
      subaccount:
        existing_destinations_policy: update
        destinations:
          - Name: CAP_DocAI_Callback
            Description: Callback destination for CAP app
            URL: ~{srv-url}/callback/notify
            Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
            TokenServiceInstanceName: docai-cap-xsuaa
            TokenServiceKeyName: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
            HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  build-parameters:
    no-source: tru

A few things worth calling out:

~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/notify
The URL is not hardcoded — it is resolved dynamically at deployment time using the srv-api provided parameters of your CAP server module. This means the destination always points to the correct URL regardless of which BTP space or region you deploy to, making the descriptor fully portable across landscapes.

OAuth2ClientCredentials
The callback destination is secured with OAuth2 Client Credentials, backed by the XSUAA instance of your CAP application. When Document AI calls the callback URL, it first fetches a Bearer token from XSUAA using the client credentials stored in docai-cap-scanner-xsuaa-key, and includes it in the Authorization header of every request — both the CSRF preflight GET and the actual callback POST. Your CAP application’s standard XSUAA-based authentication middleware validates this token automatically.

existing_destinations_policy: update
This ensures that redeployments update the destination in place rather than failing on a name conflict. Safe to use in CI/CD pipelines.

HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
Marks the destination as dynamically resolvable, which is required for runtime URL resolution via the destination service.

Configuring the Outbound Channel in SAP Document AI

Once deployed, the destination is available in your BTP subaccount and can be referenced directly from the SAP Document AI configuration UI. Navigate to Channels in Document AI and create an Outbound Channel ensuring the toggle for "Use SAP BTP Destination" is enabled.

 

Once saved, go to your schema, navigate to the Outbound Channel Configuration and add the just configured channel and toggle on/off the inclusion of your extraction results. Specifying the Document Status in which the channel shall be triggered allows you to either immediately forward the notification once the extraction is done (statusReview Needed) or only after a human validation (e.g. status Confirmed) .

With both the destination provisioned automatically via MTA and the outbound channel registered in the Document AI schema UI, the entire integration is reproducible from a single mbt build && cf deploy — no manual post-deployment steps required.

Conclusion

SAP Document AI is a powerful extraction service, but its fixed CSRF preflight behavior creates a real integration challenge when connecting to CAP applications or Integration Flows. By hooking into CAP’s bootstrap lifecycle and registering a focused, well-structured Express middleware, we can elegantly satisfy Document AI’s handshake requirements without patching CAP internals or weakening the application’s overall security posture.

The result is a clean two-layer architecture: the middleware owns the CSRF token lifecycle at the HTTP layer, and the callback service owns the business logic of matching jobs and processing extraction results — each with a single, clear responsibility.

This pattern is also broadly reusable: any time you need to expose a raw HTTP endpoint in a CAP application that bypasses the standard framework middleware (for webhooks, health checks, or third-party callbacks with unusual requirements), cds.on('bootstrap') is your entry point.

Happy building! 🚀

entry.title: Demystifying Model Context Protocol (MCP), Part 2: From Concept to Code
entry.content:
Recap and Next Steps

In Part 1, we laid the foundation for Model Context Protocol. We learnt MCP as an open standard that lets AI applications (hosts) talk to external systems through MCP clients and servers, instead of every team inventing a custom “tool API” for each integration.  We also focused on WHAT, WHY and WHEN of MCP. I strongly recommend going through Part 1 to understand the concepts of MCP and how it helps to streamline the integrations in agentic solutions.

In Part 2, we will focus more on:

  1. Building an MCP server
  2. Exposing tools
  3. Integrating with backend enterprise systems (e-commerce application)
  4. Debugging and Testing locally
  5. Deploying on cloud (Business Technology Platform)

To make this concrete, we’ll turn the ideas from Part 1 into something you can actually run end to end: A commerce MCP server that wraps Commerce OData APIs as agent‑ready tools.
By the end of Part 2, you will not just understand what a remote MCP server is, you will have one running on your local machine and can be deployed on cloud.

Commerce MCP Server (Architecture and Building Blocks)

The commerce-mcp-server project is a Python‑based MCP server built on FastMCP that exposes a set of tools wrapping Commerce (or any CAP‑based commerce) OData APIs. Instead of calling your Commerce APIs directly from the your agentic solution, the MCP server sits in the middle and offers agent‑ready tools like get_products, get_retailers etc. that are easy for MCP‑aware clients to discover and use. This is the concrete “MCP server” instance that brings the abstract concepts from Part 1 to life.

Building blocks of Commerce MCP Server

  1. Agentic Solutions + MCP Client: This is basically your Enterprise Agentic AI solution which embeds MCP client that talks to the commerce MCP server on behalf of the LLM.  MCP client discovers available tools exposed by the commerce MCP server and routes the LLM's request for data to the MCP server.
  2. Commerce MCP Server: MCP server is the application or a program which exposes the e-commerce capabilities to the AI Agent application through MCP standards.
  3. Tools: Tools are actions that the MCP server can perform. For example: "Get Product List", "Create Cart", "Add Product to Cart" etc. Tools gives host applications a structured set of capabilities.
  4. Transport Layer: MCP uses standard communication like STDIO for local and HTTP with Server‑Sent Events for remote servers.

The diagram below gives a overview of how commerce-mcp-server acts as a facade for the agentic AI solutions.

Jumping into the Code: How to Run It Yourself

Now that we’ve seen the architecture and building blocks, let’s get this MCP server running on your own machine and subaccount. The GitHub project already contains a detailed, step‑by‑step guide. in this section I will highlight the key choices and pitfalls, then point you to the exact README sections to follow.

Step 1: Pre-requisites

Before touching any code, verify the basics as called out in the sections

  1. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#pre-requisites
  2. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#local-set-up 

If you just want to play with the MCP server without touching BTP, the README’s “Option 1: Mock Backend (no BTP required)” path lets you skip the subaccount and destination setup for now.

Step 2: Decide - mock backend or live backend

The next decision point is: Do you want to hit real Commerce data, or just see the MCP server in action?

  1. For a quick start: Use mock backend as mentioned in the README file.

  2. For an end‑to‑end scenario against BTP:

    1. Use live backend.

    2. Then follow the VCAP SERVICES section to load service credentials into VCAP_SERVICES locally.

If this is your first time, start with Option 1 to understand MCP’s flow. Then come back and switch to Option 2 once your Commerce OData APIs are ready.

Step 3: Start MCP Server

Just start the MCP server to get started - https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#mcp-server-on-local 

You can see the MCP inspector where you can see the tools and also test them.

 

The good news is: You don’t have to memorize any of these steps. The README file in the repository is intentionally written as a step‑by‑step playbook: from prerequisites and local setup, through mock vs live backend, all the way to BTP deployment and curl‑based debugging. Once you understand the high‑level choices we just walked through, you can simply follow the README top‑to‑bottom and you will end up with a working commerce MCP server and MCP Inspector showing all tools on your local.

Wrapping Up Part 2

At this point, you have seen how the commerce MCP server maps the MCP concepts from Part 1 into a concrete implementation. The architecture diagram and building blocks show you where the MCP server sits between MCP clients and your Commerce OData APIs. Rather than duplicating every command here, the README gives you a detailed, step‑by‑step path to go from zero to a running MCP server locally with mock data or against live Commerce services and finally deployed on BTP.

My recommendation: Read this blog once to get the mental model, then switch to the README and follow it top‑to‑bottom. If you do that, you will finish Part 2 not just with a theoretical understanding, but with a working commerce MCP server, tools like get_products, get_categories etc. exposed via MCP and at least one client (MCP Inspector, helper script, or curl) successfully talking to it.

In Part-3 of the series, we will move from standalone MCP server to integrating it with MCP client. We will create an agent and integrate MCP server, all using Joule Studio.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol
  2. https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/architecture 
  3. https://gofastmcp.com/getting-started/quickstart

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q1-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
With this blog post, I would like to introduce a regular quarterly update series highlighting the latest innovations and enhancements in SAP Green Ledger. These updates provide an overview of new features and improvements delivered during the last quarter, helping you stay up to date with ongoing developments.

Short introduction: What is SAP Green Ledger? SAP Green Ledger supports organizations in integrating carbon into financial processes. It runs as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrates with SAP S/4HANA Cloud–based finance systems. It enables organizations to monitor and document carbon emission impacts at transaction level and link emissions to financial objects. By using established financial structures such as accounts, company codes, and controlling objects, carbon emissions can be consistently assigned to business activities and financial categories. This supports transparent and auditable reporting, as well as data-driven operational and strategic decision-making.

With Q1 2026, SAP Green Ledger continues to evolve, supporting organizations in moving beyond emissions tracking toward managing carbon as a financial dimension. The features released in the first quarter of this year bring a range of enhancements in carbon liability management, regulatory readiness, and financial integration, along with improvements that make everyday processes more transparent and flexible. This blog post covers the following new features:

  • CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities
  • Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates
  • Linking Carbon Data to Projects
  • Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems
  • Posting on Blocked Accounts
  • Reversing Manual Journal Entries
  • Further Updates and Improvements

 

CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities

One of the key highlights this quarter is the support for carbon certificate data and carbon liabilities. Carbon certificates and liabilities play a central role in regulatory frameworks such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), where companies must cover embedded emissions with certificates that carry a financial value. Since the EU CBAM came into effect in January 2026, companies that import specific goods into the EU are now required to report embedded emissions in these imported goods and build up corresponding carbon liabilities. While the actual purchase of EU CBAM certificates will only begin in 2027, organizations already need to track and value these obligations today.

To support these new requirements, the new Configure Carbon Certificate Data app provides a centralized entry point for maintaining the data required for carbon certificate handling and valuation. You can create and maintain Emissions Trading System (ETS) prices, which represent the cost of emitting one metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e). Since these prices fluctuate over time and directly impact liability valuation, maintaining them each calendar week helps ensure accuracy and alignment with market developments.

Maintaining Weekly EU ETS Prices (Illustrative Values Only)

In addition, you can display available company codes and access yearly CBAM factors. These factors determine the portion of embedded emissions that must be covered by CBAM certificates. By bringing ETS pricing, company context, and CBAM factors together, the app creates a consistent foundation that connects configuration with carbon data collection, liability calculation, and journal entry processing.

The flow is further supported by enhancements in the Manage Carbon Collections app. When creating a carbon collection, you can now indicate whether carbon certificates are required and maintain relevant information such as the number of certificates to surrender. This ensures that certificate requirements are captured consistently at the point of data entry. The information can then be used in later processing steps.

Carbon Certificate -relevant Carbon Collection

Building on this, the new Manage Carbon Liabilities app provides a dedicated space to view and evaluate carbon liabilities resulting from carbon collections that require certificates. You can analyze both the number of certificates to surrender as well as their corresponding monetary value based on ETS prices, reflecting the financial obligations associated with embedded emissions.

A key part of this process is fair value calculation. The system now calculates the fair value of carbon liabilities automatically for the current calendar year. This calculation is triggered whenever ETS prices are created, updated, or deleted, as well as when carbon collections requiring certificates are posted to the journal. Fair values are calculated by multiplying the number of certificates to surrender by the latest quarterly average ETS price. The calculated fair value covers the period from January 1 to the selected posting date within the year. At the same time, you can still trigger fair value calculations manually. This allows you to recalculate values when needed while keeping posting logic consistent.

Transparency is further improved through integration with journal entries as in the Manage Journal Entries app, you can review data originating from carbon collections. This includes, next to the line items for the collected GHG emissions, CBAM embedded emissions and certificate quantities now.

Journal Entries for CBAM-relevant Carbon Collection

Together, these capabilities provide greater transparency and control over how carbon liabilities are valued and reflected in financial processes. For more information, check SAP Help: Carbon Certificate Management. Looking ahead, we will continue to further develop support in this area. While the current focus is on supporting the calculation and management of carbon liabilities, future developments will address the next phase of EU CBAM. Starting from 2027, companies will be required to purchase CBAM certificates to cover their reported emissions. To support this, SAP Green Ledger will be extended with dedicated certificate management capabilities.

 

Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates

To support CBAM-relevant journal entry postings, account determination for carbon certificates has been enhanced with the Determine Mandatory Accounts app (formerly Manage Carbon-Only Accounts). It enables you to assign specific CBAM accounts to charts of accounts and company codes, supporting transactions such as liability recognition and fair value adjustments. This setup is mandatory for organizations subject to EU CBAM requirements. The app provides predefined CBAM account types for assets, liabilities, and operating expenses, which are typically maintained in a connected finance system and used in SAP Green Ledger. Postings to these accounts are generated automatically, ensuring alignment with underlying carbon processes.

Accounts Assignment for EU CBAM Accounts

Check out SAP Help for more information: Assign Carbon Certificate Accounts.

 

Linking Carbon Data to Projects

Carbon data can now be linked to projects and WBS (work breakdown structure) elements. A new WBS element field is available across the apps Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, Display Account Balances, and the Carbon Data Dashboard. This allows you to link carbon emissions directly to specific WBS elements. For that, project and WBS master data can now be imported from a connected finance system using our master data import capabilities and viewed in the new Display Projects app. This enhancement enables you to gain a more structured and detailed view of carbon data and makes it possible to analyze emissions at the level of individual projects and WBS elements.

Example Project in the Display Projects App

 

Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems

Another improvement is the increased flexibility in system integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management systems. Before, you could only connect one business system with the role SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to a SAP Green Ledger system. Now it is possible to create multiple business systems with that role. This allows you to work with more than one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system, for example when importing carbon data from different sources. At the same time, each finance business system can be associated with only one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system connected to a SAP Green Ledger system. This clear assignment ensures consistent and well-structured data integration. The setup is handled through business system configuration, where you define the system, assign its role, and maintain integration details such as the connected finance system.

 

Posting on Blocked Accounts

Posting behaviour has been made more flexible in SAP Green Ledger. Carbon collections can now be posted on accounts even if they are currently marked as blocked in the system. This is particularly relevant in scenarios where the account status may differ between SAP Green Ledger and the connected finance system. This can occur because account data in SAP Green Ledger is not updated in real time but is instead based on data imports from the finance system. As a result, the status in SAP Green Ledger may not always reflect the most current state.

Warning Message when Posting on a Blocked Account

Against this background, postings can still be carried out even if an account is indicated as blocked in SAP Green Ledger at a given point in time. Before posting, a warning message informs you if the account is marked as blocked. To ensure accuracy, you can verify the actual status of the account directly in the connected finance business system. Overall, this adjustment allows you to continue processes without interruption while still maintaining transparency and control over account usage.

 

Reversing Manual Journal Entries

You can now reverse manual journal entries in our Manage Journal Entries app, which makes it easier to correct postings and maintain accurate carbon data records. Reversing journal entries is possible if certain conditions are met, such as having the appropriate source type and not having been reversed previously. Once reversed, the system automatically creates corresponding journal entries in which debits and credits are swapped, ensuring that the financial impact is fully offset while preserving traceability.

The reversal information, including status, who initiated the reversal and when as well as the reversed journal entries, is available in the journal entry document, allowing you to track changes and maintain compliance with accounting standards. This enhancement helps ensure data accuracy while supporting controlled correction processes within SAP Green Ledger. For more details, have a look at SAP Help: Reverse Journal Entries.

 

Further Updates and Improvements

In addition to the key capabilities described above, several additional enhancements have been introduced to improve usability and transparency:

  • A new Journal Entry Source Type field is now available in key apps such as Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances and provides visibility into how journal entries were created (e.g., through allocation processes, liability valuation, carbon collection postings, imports, or manual entries and reversals).
  • Updates to the allocation functionality include the renaming of the allocation type Manual Allocation to Periodic Allocation and related UI adjustments.
  • Certain allocation options have been streamlined, such as the removal of the allocation type Account Balance Allocation from the Display Account Balances app.
  • External commenting is now available in the User Guide, enabling direct feedback and collaboration, see also Blog Post.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.
